Name: curvature_distortion_rate_mutual_info
Title: The Curvature of Distortion-Rate Functions in Model Compression: Insights from Mutual Information
Short Hypothesis: The curvature of the distortion-rate function, quantified by mutual information between representations, reveals regions of high redundancy and optimal bit allocation in model compression, providing a theoretical framework to approach information-theoretic limits.
Related Work: Existing work on model compression focuses on practical techniques like quantization and pruning but lacks a thorough theoretical analysis of the distortion-rate function's curvature. Related papers on mutual information optimization and knowledge distillation provide foundational insights but do not explicitly address curvature or redundancy. This proposal builds on these foundations by introducing a novel analysis of curvature and its implications for bit efficiency.
Abstract: Model compression techniques like quantization and pruning are essential for deploying deep neural networks in resource-constrained environments. While practical methods have been extensively studied, the fundamental limits of compression remain poorly understood. This paper addresses this gap by analyzing the curvature of the distortion-rate function, quantified through mutual information between representations. We hypothesize that the curvature reveals regions of high redundancy and optimal bit allocation, providing a theoretical framework to approach information-theoretic limits. We derive achievable bounds on the distortion-rate function and introduce estimators for mutual information to quantify task-relevant information. Experiments validate our approach, demonstrating that curvature analysis improves bit efficiency in practical compression techniques. Our results bridge the gap between theoretical limits and practical methods, offering new insights into the fundamental trade-offs in model compression.
Experiments: Derive achievable bounds on the distortion-rate function using mutual information as the distortion measure.
Develop estimators for mutual information between original and compressed representations, including bias/variance analysis.
Analyze the curvature of the distortion-rate function to identify regions of high redundancy and optimal bit allocation.
Evaluate the efficiency of practical compression techniques (quantization and pruning) relative to the theoretical limits.
Compare the proposed approach to state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNet) using metrics such as compression ratio, accuracy, and mutual information.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed mutual information estimators may suffer from high variance, limiting their practical utility.
The curvature analysis assumes specific probabilistic assumptions, which may not hold for all datasets or models.
The theoretical bounds may be difficult to achieve in practice due to hardware constraints or implementation complexities.
The approach may require significant computational resources for large-scale models and datasets.
